subject: Physics
solution:
Kinetic energy $_{\text {(rotational) }} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I} \omega^2$
Kinetic energy $_{\text {(translational) }} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Mv}^2$
$\begin{aligned}
& (\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{R} \omega) \\
& \text { M.I. }_{\text {(initial) }} \mathrm{I}_{\text {ring }}=\mathrm{MR}^2 ; \omega_{\text {initial }}=\omega \\
& \text { M.I. } _{\text { (new) }}{\mathrm{I}^{\prime}}{ }_{\text {(system) }}=\mathrm{MR}^2+2 \mathrm{mR}^2 \\
& \omega_{(\text {system) }}^{\prime}=\frac{M \omega}{M+2 m} \\
&
\end{aligned}$
Solving we get loss in $\mathrm{K.E.}$
$=\frac{\mathrm{Mm}}{(\mathrm{M}+2 \mathrm{~m})} \omega^2 \mathrm{R}^2$

⇒mgh=12mv02+12×25ma2×v02a2
⇒gh=12v02+15v02
⇒gh=710v02

From triangle, sin θ=hl
Then h=l sin θ
⇒l sin θ=710v02 g
Thus, the sphere will travel distance, l=710v02 g sin θ.

subject: Physics
solution:
Moment of inertia of the rod about axis $AB$ passing through one of its ends:
$I_{\text{rod}} = \dfrac{ML^2}{3} = \dfrac{40 \times (3)^2}{3} = \dfrac{40 \times 9}{3} = 120\text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$

Moment of inertia of the solid sphere about the same axis $AB$, using the parallel axis theorem $I = I_{\text{cm}} + md^2$:
Mass $m = 10\text{ kg}$, radius $R$, distance from the centre of the sphere to axis $AB$ is $d = 3\text{ m}$.

$I_{\text{sphere}} = \dfrac{2}{5}mR^2 + md^2 = \dfrac{2}{5}(10)R^2 + 10(3)^2 = 4R^2 + 90$

Equating the two moments of inertia:
$120 = 4R^2 + 90$
$4R^2 = 30$
$R^2 = \dfrac{15}{2}$
$R = \sqrt{\dfrac{15}{2}}$

Comparing with $R = \sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha}{2}}$:
$\alpha = 15$

Hence, $\alpha = 15$.

subject: Physics
solution:
Angular momentum $\propto \frac{1}{\text { Angular frequency }} \propto$ Kinetic energy $\Rightarrow \vec{L}=\frac{\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E} .}{\mathrm{w}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{L}_1}{\mathrm{~L}_2}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}_1}{\mathrm{w}_1}\right) \times \frac{\mathrm{w}_2}{\mathrm{KE}_2}=4 \Rightarrow \mathrm{L}_2=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{4}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Let speed of cylinder upon reaching the bottom of the inclined plane be v.

Here, gain in kinetic energy=loss in potential energy

So, 12Iω2+12mv2=mglsinθ

⇒12mr22ω2+12mv2=mglsin30°

For pure rolling, v=rω

⇒12mr22vr2+12mv2=mglsin30°

⇒14mv2+12mv2=mglsin30°⇒34mv2=mgl2⇒v=4gl6

Putting the values, we have

⇒v=4×10×0.66=2 m s-1

subject: Physics
solution:
M.I. of complete disc about its centre $O$. $$ I_{\mathrm{Total}}=\frac{1}{2} M R^2 $$
Mass of circular hole (removed) $$ =\frac{M}{4} \quad\left(\text { As } M=\pi R^2 t \therefore M \propto R^2\right) $$ M.I. of removed hole about its own axis $$ =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{M}{4}\right)\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^2=\frac{1}{32} M R^2 $$ M.I. of removed hole about $O^{\prime}$ $$ \begin{aligned} I_{\text {removed hole }} & =I_{\mathrm{cm}}+m x^2 \\ & =\frac{M R^2}{32}+\frac{M}{4}\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{M R^2}{32}+\frac{M R^2}{16}=\frac{3 M R^2}{32} \end{aligned} $$ M.I. of complete disc can also be written as $I_{\text {Total }}=I_{\text {removed hole }}+I_{\text {remaining disc }}$ $I_{\text {Total }}=\frac{3 M R^2}{32}+I_{\text {remaining disc }}$ From eq. (i) and (ii), $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} M R^2=\frac{3 M R^2}{32}+I_{\text {remaining disc }} \\ & \Rightarrow I_{\text {remaining disc }} \\ & \quad=\frac{M R^2}{2}-\frac{3 M R^2}{32}=\left(\frac{13}{32}\right) M R^2 \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
solution:
It is given that the force F→=-Pk^

The coordinates of the origin is (0,0).

The coordinates of the point given is  (2,-3)

The displacement vector is 

r→=(0-2)i^+(0+3)j^=-2i^+3j^

Hence, the torque is 

τ→=r→×F→=(-2i^+3j^)×(-Pk^)

⇒τ→=-2Pj^-3Pi^=P(ai^+bj^)

So, it can be written

a=-3, b=-2

∴  ab=32⇒ so x=3

subject: Physics
solution:
Let mass of the larger triangle $=\mathrm{M}$ Side of larger triangle $=\ell$ Moment of inertia of larger triangle $=\mathrm{ma}^{2}$

Mass of smaller triangle $=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{4}$

Length of smaller triangle $=\frac{\ell}{2}$

Moment of inertia of removed triangle $=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{4}\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}\right)^{2}$

$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{I}_{\text {removed }}}{\mathrm{I}_{\text {original }}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{M}}{4}}{\mathrm{M}} \cdot \frac{\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}\right)^{2}}{(\mathrm{a})^{2}}$

$I_{\text {removed }}=\frac{I_{0}}{16}$

$\mathrm{So}, \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{0}-\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{16}=\frac{15 \mathrm{I}_{0}}{16}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Using the parallel axis theorem for radius of gyration:
$k^2 = k_{cm}^2 + d^2$ where $k_{cm}$ is the radius of gyration about the center of mass and $d$ is the distance from the center.
For a solid sphere:
$I_{center} = \frac{2}{5}MR^2$, so $k_{cm}^2 = \frac{2}{5}R^2$
With $R = 10$ cm and $d = 15$ cm:
$k^2 = \frac{2}{5}(10)^2 + (15)^2 = 40 + 225 = 265$
Since $k = \sqrt{n}$, we have $n = 265$.
